title: Filing Relief for Natural Disasters Act
short_title: Filing Relief for Natural Disasters Act
plain_summary: This law helps people who live in areas hit by natural disasters get more time to file their taxes. Right now, only disasters declared by the federal government can delay tax deadlines, but this law lets state governors and the mayor of Washington DC also request deadline extensions for disasters they declare in their areas. The law also gives people twice as much extra time to file their taxes after a disaster, increasing the automatic extension from 60 days to 120 days. This means people dealing with floods, fires, explosions, and other emergencies will have more time to handle their taxes while they recover from the disaster.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Filing Relief for Natural Disasters Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Modification of Rules for Postponing Certain Deadlines by Reason of Disaster
sections.text: This section makes changes to the tax law about delaying tax deadlines when disasters happen. The first change allows the Secretary of the Treasury to delay federal tax deadlines when a state governor declares a disaster. The Secretary must talk with the head of the Federal Emergency Management Agency first. The governor of a state or the mayor of Washington DC can ask in writing for this help. A qualified state declared disaster means any natural disaster like hurricanes, tornadoes, storms, flooding, tsunamis, earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, landslides, mudslides, snowstorms, or droughts. It also includes fires, floods, or explosions no matter what caused them. The governor or mayor must decide that the damage is bad enough to need tax deadline relief. For this law, a state includes Washington DC, Puerto Rico, the Virgin Islands, Guam, American Samoa, and the Northern Mariana Islands. The second change extends the longest time tax deadlines can be delayed from sixty days to one hundred twenty days. These changes only apply to disaster declarations made after this law is passed.
